stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( ([[p]])y^{2}\,dx + ([[q]])x^{2}\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the boundary of the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \), oriented counterclockwise.
solution: With \( P=([[p]])y^{2} \) and \( Q=([[q]])x^{2} \), Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_D (\partial_x Q - \partial_y P)\,dA = \iint_D (2([[q]])x - 2([[p]])y)\,dA \) over \( [0,[[W]]]\times[0,[[H]]] \), which equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the orientation, giving the negative of the correct circulation.
This adds \( \partial_x Q + \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting, using the wrong Green's-theorem integrand.
This swaps the roles of \( P \) and \( Q \) in the integrand.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( ([[p]])y\,dx + ([[q]])xy\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the boundary of the triangle with vertices \( (0,0) \), \( ([[W]],0) \), and \( (0,[[W]]) \), oriented counterclockwise. The hypotenuse lies on \( y = [[W]] - x \).
solution: With \( P=([[p]])y \) and \( Q=([[q]])xy \), Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_D (\partial_x Q - \partial_y P)\,dA = \iint_D \big(([[q]])y - ([[p]])\big)\,dA \) over the triangle \( 0\le x\le [[W]],\ 0\le y\le [[W]]-x \), which equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the orientation, giving the negative of the correct circulation.
This misapplies a rectangle-style shortcut area formula to a triangular region.
This forgets to combine the two terms under ONE double integral (computes each with a mismatched bound) rather than integrating \( qy - p \) directly over the triangle.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use Green's theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \left( ([[p]])y\,dx + ([[q]])x\,dy \right) \), where \( C \) is the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=([[R]])^{2} \), oriented counterclockwise.
solution: With \( P=([[p]])y \) and \( Q=([[q]])x \), Green's theorem gives \( \oint_C P\,dx+Q\,dy = \iint_D (\partial_x Q - \partial_y P)\,dA = \iint_D \big(([[q]])-([[p]])\big)\,dA \). Since the integrand is the CONSTANT \( ([[q]])-([[p]]) \), this is that constant times the disk's area \( \pi([[R]])^{2} \), giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the orientation, giving the negative of the correct circulation.
This adds \( \partial_x Q + \partial_y P \) instead of subtracting, using the wrong Green's-theorem integrand.
This double counts, as if traversing the circle twice.
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct value.
This subtracts a spurious \( \pi \) from the correct value.
